stem: A hospital uses a lumbar puncture CSF analysis to screen for meningitis. The test has a sensitivity of 92% and a specificity of 97%. The prevalence of meningitis in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.1% and 5.7%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has meningitis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(meningitis | positive test) ranges from 0.397 to 0.65 as prevalence varies from 2.1% to 5.7%. Point estimate at 3.9% prevalence: 0.554.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.03.
The confidence interval [0.397, 0.65] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (92%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
